App_1bgz4xw: Babel + Vite plugin to extract strings from source (marker %s and component <Translate> ) and generate/sync the JSON translation tables. Language files are grouped under src (not in public, to avoid serving injectable JSON) and loaded lazily one at a time: switching language at runtime downloads only the required chunk, without bloating the initial bundle with unused languages.
InstallSection_19ok788: . The default language file is created and kept up to date automatically by the sync command: it should not be written by hand, only translated if needed.
InstallSection_1c60rw8: Register the viteTranslate plugin in vite.config.js: you need to point it to the language files folder and the default language. The plugin handles both extracting the strings to translate via Babel and generating the virtual module that lists the available languages.
InstallSection_1gk4ndc: Before the production build, sync the JSON tables with all the strings found in the source by running the command as a "prebuild" step: this ensures the default language is always complete.
InstallSection_1rh2slk: Wrap the app in TranslateContainer, specifying the initial language to load: it exposes the context used by Translate and the other hooks to read the current translation table. In development the fallback stays embedded in the compiled code, so the app already shows the translated text without having to run other commands.
InstallSection_1uw15rr: Here's how src looks once the plugin is configured: locale is a subfolder like the others, indicated by localeDir, containing one JSON file per language.
InstallSection_2045ru: To add a new language, create an empty file named after the chosen tag, then rerun the sync command: it appends all the missing keys with a null value and reports in the log which ones remain to be translated. Just replace those nulls with the translated text.
InstallSection_2g6xwo: Every .json file found in localeDir automatically becomes available: useAvailableLanguages() lists it and TranslateContainer loads it lazily on demand, without needing to register it anywhere else.
InstallSection_33xaw9: The same keys appear, at the same time, in the default language file too: never as null there, but grouped below the same separator line as long as they remain untranslated in at least one other language. That's a handy opportunity: you can copy that block (real text, not null) and paste it into an LLM to have it translated, then paste the answer back in place of the nulls in the destination language file.
InstallSection_b6ycgm: Install the package in your Vite + React project: React and Vite are peer dependencies, so they must already be present.
InstallSection_gh1150: Simply running this command, even on a brand-new project with no other language files present, generates the first language table: the one for the language set as the default language in
InstallSection_k23va3: By replacing every null with the translated text (keeping any %s unchanged), the file is complete, ready to be used as an available language.
InstallSection_lx2wuc: . It's exactly the file that localeDir must already contain at first startup, as required in Dev run.
InstallSection_qiokp6: Right after the command, the file already contains all the keys found in the source, but untranslated (null value): below the separator line you'll find the exact list of what's missing.
InstallSection_u1ivbq: Before the first startup, localeDir must already exist as a folder and contain the default language file: TranslateContainer looks for it immediately and, if it's missing, loading fails. If you haven't generated it yet, run the command described in Language build once: at the end you'll already have a folder containing the default language table, ready to be read.
InstallSection_xsiph: Each language is a JSON file inside localeDir, with the same name as its tag
PlaceholderExample_1ebkbf3: The name will be used in the greeting
StrengthsSection_13j7ggd: Text marked directly in the source. No keys to create or manage by hand.
StrengthsSection_17k5tb2: New languages detected automatically. Just add a JSON file to the languages folder, without registering it anywhere else in the code.
StrengthsSection_19otd0h: Automatic fallback in development. Until a translation is ready, the original text stays visible, never an empty string or an error.
StrengthsSection_1alvua3: Lazy language loading. Switching language at runtime downloads only the required chunk, without bloating the initial bundle with unused languages.
StrengthsSection_1og57qy: Translations survive renames. If the same text only changes id, the sync inherits the translation already done instead of starting it over from scratch.
StrengthsSection_1ppjcnx: Automatic table sync. A single command updates all languages and flags what remains to be translated.
StrengthsSection_orzg8c: Works on Vite 7 and Vite 8 alike. Same code for both: on Vite 7 it runs on Rollup (build) and esbuild (dev), on Vite 8 it runs on Rolldown (Rust bundler) and Oxc (compiler), no different configuration needed.
StrengthsSection_s5g6mb: Language files kept safe in src, not in public. Avoids serving JSON tables that can be modified client-side.
ZZZ_snippetList_1bsapvw: The useAvailableLanguages() hook exposes the BCP 47 tags found in localeDir (no loading, just the list); the TranslateContainer context exposes id (current language), debug (passed to TranslateContainer) and proposeNewLanguage to request lazy loading of another language at runtime, with onStart/onDone/onError callbacks to track the outcome.
ZZZ_snippetList_1tp9az4: Format t={[text, arg1, arg2, ...]} to interpolate variables into the translated text.
ZZZ_snippetList_fweqm6: The <Translate> component wraps static text marked with %s, replaced at build time with the translation id and its fallback.
ZZZ_snippetList_vbgexs: <Translate> returns React nodes: it cannot be used in HTML attributes that require a plain string, such as placeholder, aria-label or title. In these cases you need the useTranslateString() hook, which resolves the same translation but returns a primitive string. These attributes are not visible as on-screen text: to see them translated, inspect the element with F12.
